So every business function has bought agents, and the IT department, which is our buyer, now has hundreds, if not thousands, of different agents that need to be managed.
And so the hot topic today is how do you have a control plan, or how do you have a way to manage this agent?
We call it agents gone wild.

And so when we meet with God, and that's what I was doing right before this, there's always a conversation about, okay, so we're talking to Google, Microsoft, and Amazon about their agentic management or platforms or whatever, what makes you different?
And for us, it's the fact that we've combined our ability to orchestrate agents with a deterministic workflow engine.
And so it's an engine that we spent four years.
has more than 20,000 different mini capabilities inside.
And effectively, our view is that the agents should be part of the workflow, not the workflow should be part of the agents, especially as you get to enterprise scale.
And so an example of that is a finance process.
You want a finance process to work exactly the same way every time, no matter what.
But as part of that finance process, you may want to employ an agent to read a document.
That's a good place where LLMs can play.
You might want to employ an agent to make a phone call or to have a conversational chat with the users.
But fundamentally, the entire workflow needs to be deterministic because at the end of that, payments are dispersed and you want to work 100% of the time.
And so that's where our platform is very powerful.
has that engine combined.
so very so where our Thank

So we run our workflows where the data is, and that's part of our core intellectual property.
Whereas for the CIO, yes, they're worried about that, but what they really need is, can your platform help me manage all these agents across key business process?
Okay, so how does Elementum help companies future-proof?
thank Thank
All right.
This is a big one.
So when you build a business process in Elementum, you lay down the tracks for that business process.
And then the second step is to say which part should be agents, which part should be humans, and which part should be rules.
And so the investment in laying down the track is done once, but as new AI models come out, new capabilities come out, you're able to come in and swap them super duper easily.
Okay.
So you're not locked in to like a specific workflow even?
Not at all.
We actually, yeah, one of the words we were kicking around yesterday is that we're an open orchestration platform, right?
You're not locked into a model.
You're not locked into a data cloud.
You're not locked into the compute layer like AWS or GCP or Azure.
And so you have tons of flexibility to adapt to your workflow as the world changes.
And just one quick thing to pile on there as well is it's not just the principle of vendor lock-in.
know, like, okay, if I put all my eggs in one agent basket and then somebody else comes out with better technology, maybe I want to switch.
But it's also the fact that when you define the workflow, that's really where you're defining the business logic.
And when you think about, and this is probably too much to get into now, but sort of all of the layers of the tech stack, there are agents at all of the layers now.
And if you build business logic into the agents at a bunch of different layers of your tech stack, it just gets horribly messy from an architectural standpoint.
If you decide you want to change business logic, you don't want to have to do it all over the stack.
The business logic actually exists in the orchestration layer.
And then you're just calling out to the agents to do the thing that they're best at.
Very well said.
Yeah.
Yeah.

yeah.

My biggest thing is agents sure are going to have a role, but so are rules and so are humans.
And if you think about like, you know, the cloud computing era, everybody said everything should move to the cloud.
But then as you look at it.
But it's actually more expensive to run a server on Amazon 100% of the time than it is to have it on-prem because they need make a margin on it.
And so I see agents are the same.
Yes, you could do a lot with it, but you're going to want to use it in the right place at the right time.
And so that's what we advocate.

don't remember the exact timing, but over the next few years, all these patents are going to expire.
